content.text: Before transformers dominated language modelling, two families of autoregressive models competed: n-gram models (count-based) and recurrent neural language models (RNN-LMs). Both share the same objective — maximise the likelihood of a sequence by predicting each token given all prior tokens — but differ in how context is represented. Understanding these pre-transformer models clarifies exactly what transformers improved upon and why perplexity is the standard evaluation metric.
A language model assigns a probability to every sequence of tokens. Using the chain rule of probability:

attrs.latex: p(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1})

content.text: Training minimises the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy loss) over a corpus. Perplexity (PPL) measures how surprised the model is per token — lower is better:

A model with PPL=10 is roughly as uncertain as if it were choosing uniformly among 10 equiprobable alternatives at each step. On Penn Treebank, n-gram models achieved ~140 PPL; good LSTM-LMs reached ~55–70; transformers reached ~20–30.
N-gram models truncate the context to the last

. Probabilities are estimated from corpus counts with Laplace or Kneser-Ney smoothing to handle zero-count n-grams. The fundamental limitation is sparsity: longer contexts (larger n) lead to exponentially more n-grams, most of which never appear in training data.
The choice of tokenisation unit determines the model's vocabulary size, sequence length, and the kinds of patterns it must learn:
Higher (more steps to span a sentence)

An LSTM-based LM (Mikolov et al., 2010 for RNN; Sundermeyer et al., 2012 for LSTM) replaces the fixed n-gram context with a recurrent hidden state that theoretically summarises the entire history. Each token is embedded, passed through one or more LSTM layers, and the output is projected to a softmax over the vocabulary.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class CharLM(nn.Module):
    """
    Character-level LSTM language model.
    Input: integer token indices in {0, ..., vocab_size-1}.
    Output: logits over vocabulary for each position.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        vocab_size: int,
        emb_dim: int = 128,
        hidden_dim: int = 512,
        n_layers: int = 2,
        dropout: float = 0.5,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_dim)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(
            emb_dim, hidden_dim, n_layers,
            batch_first=True, dropout=dropout if n_layers > 1 else 0.0,
        )
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, vocab_size)
        # Weight tying: output projection shares weights with embedding
        self.proj.weight = self.embedding.weight
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.n_layers = n_layers

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,    # [B, T] integer token indices
        hidden=None,
    ):
        emb = self.drop(self.embedding(x))  # [B, T, emb_dim]
        out, hidden = self.lstm(emb, hidden) # [B, T, hidden_dim]
        logits = self.proj(self.drop(out))   # [B, T, vocab_size]
        return logits, hidden

    def init_hidden(self, batch_size: int, device):
        h = torch.zeros(self.n_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim, device=device)
        c = torch.zeros(self.n_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim, device=device)
        return h, c


def lm_loss(
    model: CharLM,
    x: torch.Tensor,   # [B, T+1] token sequences (input and target offset by 1)
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Teacher-forcing cross-entropy loss.
    Input  x[:, :-1] (tokens 0..T-1), targets x[:, 1:] (tokens 1..T).
    """
    inp = x[:, :-1]      # [B, T]
    target = x[:, 1:]    # [B, T]
    logits, _ = model(inp)  # [B, T, V]
    B, T, V = logits.shape
    return F.cross_entropy(logits.reshape(B * T, V), target.reshape(B * T))

Weight tying (Press & Wolf, 2017) shares the embedding matrix with the output projection. Intuitively, both layers relate words to the same latent semantic space — the embedding maps tokens to vectors, and the output projection maps vectors back to token probabilities using the same geometry. Tying reduces parameters substantially (by

def compute_perplexity(
    model: CharLM,
    tokens: torch.Tensor,   # [1, T+1]
    device: torch.device,
) -> float:
    """Evaluate per-token perplexity on a sequence."""
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        loss = lm_loss(model, tokens.to(device))
    return loss.exp().item()


def sample_text(
    model: CharLM,
    seed_tokens: list,          # list of initial token indices
    idx_to_char: dict,
    n_chars: int = 200,
    temperature: float = 1.0,
    top_k: int = 0,             # 0 = no top-k filtering
    device: torch.device = torch.device('cpu'),
) -> str:
    """
    Autoregressively sample characters from the LM.
    temperature < 1: sharper (more deterministic)
    temperature > 1: flatter (more random)
    top_k > 0: only sample from top-k most probable tokens
    """
    model.eval()
    x = torch.tensor(seed_tokens, dtype=torch.long, device=device).unsqueeze(0)  # [1, L]
    hidden = model.init_hidden(1, device)
    generated = list(seed_tokens)

    with torch.no_grad():
        for _ in range(n_chars):
            logits, hidden = model(x, hidden)    # [1, 1, V]
            logits = logits[0, -1, :]            # [V]
            logits = logits / temperature
            if top_k > 0:
                topk_vals, _ = torch.topk(logits, top_k)
                threshold = topk_vals[-1]
                logits[logits < threshold] = float('-inf')
            probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            next_token = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            generated.append(next_token)
            x = torch.tensor([[next_token]], dtype=torch.long, device=device)

    return ''.join(idx_to_char.get(t, '?') for t in generated)


def nucleus_sample(
    logits: torch.Tensor,
    p: float = 0.9,
) -> int:
    """
    Top-p (nucleus) sampling: sample from the smallest set of tokens
    whose cumulative probability ≥ p.
    """
    probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = torch.sort(probs, descending=True)
    cumprobs = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    # Find where cumulative prob first exceeds p
    cutoff = (cumprobs - sorted_probs >= p).sum().item()  # keep tokens 0..cutoff
    top_probs = sorted_probs[:cutoff + 1]
    top_idx   = sorted_idx[:cutoff + 1]
    # Sample from the nucleus
    chosen_local = torch.multinomial(top_probs, 1).item()
    return top_idx[chosen_local].item()
For long sequences, backpropagation through time is truncated to
steps. The hidden state is carried forward across chunks (detached from the graph) to maintain long-range context without unbounded memory.
Apply the same dropout mask at each timestep (not fresh per step). This prevents the model from relying on the randomness of different masks at different steps.
Activation regularisation penalises large activations; temporal AR penalises large differences between consecutive hidden states — encouraging smooth temporal dynamics.
Clip gradients by global norm (typically 1.0 or 5.0) to stabilise LSTM training, which is prone to exploding gradients.

def train_step_tbptt(
    model: CharLM,
    x_chunk: torch.Tensor,   # [B, chunk_size+1] tokens for this chunk
    hidden,                   # detached hidden state from previous chunk
    optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
    clip_norm: float = 1.0,
) -> tuple:
    """One truncated BPTT step: process a chunk, clip gradients, step."""
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss = lm_loss(model, x_chunk)  # cross-entropy over chunk
    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), clip_norm)
    optimizer.step()
    # Detach hidden state: carry context forward without backprop through it
    new_hidden = tuple(h.detach() for h in hidden) if isinstance(hidden, tuple) else hidden.detach()
    return loss.item(), new_hidden

A character LM is a typist with a running mental state: after reading every character, they update their sense of where the document is going, and use that to predict what to type next. N-gram models are typists with amnesia who only remember the last 2–4 characters; LSTM-LMs have a notepad (the cell state) to write down and erase longer-term context. The softmax over 256 characters is a probability distribution over what key to press — and perplexity measures how surprised the typist is on average.

Understanding n-gram and RNN/LSTM language models is not merely historical — it clarifies the design decisions that made transformers so successful. The problems that plagued pre-transformer models (fixed context windows, vanishing gradients, slow sequential computation) map directly onto the transformer innovations (attention, residual streams, parallel training). Without understanding what came before, it is hard to appreciate why attention is so powerful or why subword tokenisation emerged as a compromise between character and word levels.

LSTM language models are also still practically useful. In constrained environments — embedded devices, edge inference, real-time streaming — a small LSTM-LM with a 50MB footprint outperforms a 7B-parameter transformer on latency and memory. Character-level models in particular see continued use in specialised domains: genomics sequences, chemical SMILES strings, source code tokenisation, and any domain where the 'tokens' are individual characters rather than English words.

N-gram model probabilities are directly derived from corpus counts. You can inspect why the model assigned a low probability to a sequence — it simply never appeared in training data. Neural LMs give probabilities without this direct interpretability.
Fast inference for small n-gram models.
A trigram lookup is a hash table operation — O(1) and microsecond-scale. For speech recognition lattice scoring or spell checking, this speed advantage over neural models is significant.
Character-level models have no OOV problem.
A model over the 256-character ASCII space handles any input without unknown token fallback. This is critical for tasks with novel proper nouns, URLs, code identifiers, or multilingual text.
LSTM-LMs are compact and trainable on small corpora.
A 2-layer LSTM-LM with 512 hidden units trains to useful quality on 10MB of domain text in minutes on a single GPU. Transformers need much more data before generalising well.
Tying the embedding and output projection removes V × emb_dim parameters (e.g. 50,000 × 256 = 12.8M), which is a substantial fraction of a small model — and it improves perplexity simultaneously.

'The cat sat on the mat' and 'The feline rested on the rug' are treated as completely independent — n-grams have no concept of word similarity. Neural models learn that cat ≈ feline from co-occurrence patterns.
LSTMs struggle with very long-range dependencies.

Despite the cell state, LSTMs in practice lose reliable access to context more than 200–300 tokens back. Coreference resolution across paragraphs, book-level consistency, and long-range grammatical agreement all suffer.

LSTM hidden states depend on the previous timestep, so the LSTM cannot be parallelised across the time dimension during training. On a 2048-length sequence, the LSTM runs 2048 sequential steps — transformers process all positions in parallel.

A 512-word sequence becomes ~2,500 characters. Training a character-level model on long documents requires much longer BPTT chunks, higher memory usage, and more training steps to see the same amount of text.

English has 170,000+ words. A 50,000-word vocabulary still maps many words to <UNK>. Subword tokenisation (BPE, SentencePiece) emerged precisely to solve this problem without going all the way to character level.
You need deterministic, interpretable probability scores (e.g., spell-checker scoring, ASR N-best rescoring).
Your corpus is small (< 1 MB) and domain-specific — n-grams fit the exact training distribution, no generalisation needed.
Inference latency must be microsecond-scale (e.g., embedded firmware, FPGAs).
You're on a device too small for a transformer (Raspberry Pi, microcontrollers, mobile CPUs).
Your data is structured sequences with local patterns (log files, musical notes, genomic sequences).
You need to train from scratch on a small domain corpus (< 10MB) in minutes.
You have enough data (> 1GB) and compute to train or fine-tune even a small transformer (GPT-2 scale).
Long-range context matters (document summarisation, code completion across files).
Real-world example: domain-specific character LM for genomics

DNA sequences consist of just 4 characters (A, C, G, T) plus N for unknown bases. A character-level LSTM-LM trains efficiently on genome sequences and can score novel sequences — useful for detecting non-natural sequences, imputing missing bases, and generating synthetic genomic data:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# DNA vocabulary: only 5 characters needed
VOCAB = {'A': 0, 'C': 1, 'G': 2, 'T': 3, 'N': 4}
IDX_TO_CHAR = {v: k for k, v in VOCAB.items()}
VOCAB_SIZE = len(VOCAB)


class DNACharLM(nn.Module):
    """Character-level LSTM language model for DNA sequences."""

    def __init__(self, vocab_size=5, emb_dim=32, hidden_dim=256, n_layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_dim)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(emb_dim, hidden_dim, n_layers,
                            batch_first=True, dropout=0.3)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, vocab_size)
        self.proj.weight = self.embedding.weight  # weight tying
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.n_layers = n_layers

    def forward(self, x, hidden=None):
        emb = self.embedding(x)                    # [B, T, emb_dim]
        out, hidden = self.lstm(emb, hidden)       # [B, T, hidden]
        logits = self.proj(out)                    # [B, T, vocab]
        return logits, hidden


def encode_dna(seq: str) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Convert DNA string to integer tensor."""
    return torch.tensor([VOCAB.get(c, VOCAB['N']) for c in seq.upper()],
                        dtype=torch.long)


def score_sequence(model: DNACharLM, seq: str, device: str = 'cpu') -> float:
    """Return log-probability per base for a DNA sequence."""
    model.eval()
    ids = encode_dna(seq).unsqueeze(0).to(device)  # [1, T]
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits, _ = model(ids[:, :-1])             # [1, T-1, V]
        targets = ids[:, 1:]                        # [1, T-1]
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.squeeze(0), targets.squeeze(0))
    return -loss.item()  # log-probability per base


def generate_dna(model: DNACharLM, seed: str = 'ATG',
                 length: int = 50, temperature: float = 1.0,
                 device: str = 'cpu') -> str:
    """Sample a DNA sequence from the model."""
    model.eval()
    x = encode_dna(seed).unsqueeze(0).to(device)  # [1, L]
    hidden = None
    generated = list(seed)
    with torch.no_grad():
        for _ in range(length):
            logits, hidden = model(x, hidden)
            logits = logits[0, -1, :] / temperature  # [V]
            probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            next_idx = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            generated.append(IDX_TO_CHAR[next_idx])
            x = torch.tensor([[next_idx]], dtype=torch.long, device=device)
    return ''.join(generated)
Not detaching the hidden state between BPTT chunks.
If you pass the raw hidden state tensor between chunks without calling .detach(), PyTorch accumulates the entire computation graph across chunks, causing memory exhaustion for long sequences.
Always choosing the most probable character leads to degenerate, repetitive text. Use temperature scaling (T=0.8–1.2) or nucleus sampling to produce diverse output.
A high-capacity model memorises training sequences and achieves zero perplexity on them. Always evaluate on a held-out test set to measure genuine generalisation.
Forgetting to clip gradients in LSTM training.
LSTMs are prone to exploding gradients, especially on long sequences. Always use torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0) before each optimizer step.
Char-LM PPL and word-LM PPL are not comparable. A char-LM has PPL over 256 characters; a word-LM has PPL over 50,000 words. Lower is better within each, but you cannot say 'char PPL 8 = word PPL 80'.
It consistently improves perplexity and reduces parameters with one line of code: self.proj.weight = self.embedding.weight. There is almost never a reason to skip it.
Use Kneser-Ney smoothing for n-gram models, not Laplace.
Laplace (add-1) over-smooths rare n-grams relative to their true frequency. Kneser-Ney consistently gives lower perplexity in real NLP applications.
Use variational dropout, not standard dropout.
Apply the same dropout mask at every timestep in each LSTM layer (variational dropout). This prevents the model from relying on mask randomness for temporal structure.
Tune BPTT chunk size to the typical context needed.
A chunk size of 128–512 is typical for text. Smaller chunks train faster but lose longer-range context. Larger chunks use more memory. Choose based on the longest dependency your task requires.
Training time and inference throughput for a 2-layer LSTM-LM with 512 hidden units (a typical small model):
Penn Treebank PPL (state of art pre-transformer)

The sequential nature of LSTMs is the fundamental throughput bottleneck. Unlike transformers, which parallelise across all time steps using attention, LSTMs require T sequential hidden state updates per sequence. This means training on the same number of tokens takes 5–10x longer than an equivalent-capacity transformer on modern GPUs.
— provides n-gram language model utilities including Kneser-Ney and Lidstone smoothing. Useful for prototyping and small corpora.
— production-grade n-gram language model library in C++ with Python bindings. Trains on hundreds of GBs of text and supports efficient on-disk queries. Used in Kaldi (ASR) and Moses (MT).
— the standard implementation, with cuDNN acceleration on GPU. Supports multi-layer, bidirectional, and dropout-between-layers configuration.
(Merity et al.) — reference implementation of the state-of-the-art LSTM language model before transformers. Implements variational dropout, AR + TAR regularisation, and QRNN layers.

# KenLM: production n-gram LM query (install: pip install https://github.com/kpu/kenlm/...)
import kenlm

model = kenlm.Model('lm.arpa')  # ARPA format n-gram file
sentence = 'The cat sat on the mat'
print(f'Log10 prob: {model.score(sentence):.4f}')   # sum of log10 P(word|context)
print(f'Perplexity: {model.perplexity(sentence):.2f}')

# Inspect per-word scores
for prob, length, oov in model.full_scores(sentence):
    print(f'  {prob:.4f} (n={length}, oov={oov})')
Language modelling maximises log p(x) = Σ log p(xₜ | x<t); perplexity exp(-1/T · Σ log p) measures average token-level uncertainty.
N-gram models are fast and interpretable but suffer exponential sparsity with longer contexts; smoothing (Laplace, Kneser-Ney) partially mitigates this.
Char-LMs avoid OOV and handle morphology but require longer sequences; word-LMs use shorter sequences but need large vocabularies and <UNK> handling.
LSTM-LMs replace fixed n-gram context with a recurrent hidden state; weight tying, variational dropout, and truncated BPTT are standard training ingredients.
Text generation uses temperature scaling, top-k, or nucleus (top-p) sampling to trade diversity for coherence; greedy decoding (temperature→0) produces repetitive output.
